GenUI shows the result. But where does the agent get the data? 🔌 Meet MCP — the Model Context Protocol. The clean mental model: MCP fetches and acts; GenUI builds the interface on top. Agent calls a tool (search_flights) → gets structured JSON → emits an A2UI blueprint (flight cards + Select) → your native app renders it → tap Select → book_flight → loop. Every action is an MCP call; every screen is a GenUI surface. Why now: donated to the Linux Foundation (Dec 2025), ~10k public servers, early-majority adoption. This is the SQL window function cheat sheet I wish people memorized. Not every function is a random trick. They fall into groups. Number rows: ROW_NUMBER, RANK, DENSE_RANK. Look around: LAG, LEAD. Calculate without collapsing: SUM, AVG, COUNT, MIN, MAX. Control the window: PARTITION BY, ORDER BY, and frames. That is the real interview pattern. If you can say which group, which order, and which nearby rows matter, most window function questions become simple. Save this before your next SQL interview or analytics PR. Many breakthrough technologies become famous only when a larger player enters the market. Real question: What matters more in technology: being first to build it, or being the company that convinces the world it matters? Trust is usually treated like a brand issue, but in modern marketing it is also tied closely to how well the business is organized behind the scenes. A company can have a good logo, a polished website, and strong visuals, but still create a weak customer experience if the systems behind the marketing are disconnected. You see this when an ad sends people to a page that does not match the offer, when an inquiry form does not trigger the right follow-up, when customer information sits in the wrong place, or when the sales conversation feels disconnected from what the marketing promised. Customers may not see the system behind the business, but they feel the result of it. They feel it when the response is slow, when the next step is unclear, when the message changes from one place to another, or when the business does not feel as organized after the inquiry as it did before. That is why marketing infrastructure matters. The website, CRM, email follow-up, lead handling, advertising, content, automation, and reporting should work together as one connected customer journey. When those pieces are aligned, the business has more control over what happens after someone shows interest. This is where modern marketing systems become valuable. They do not just help a business move faster. They help the business respond more consistently, manage leads more properly, and create a stronger experience from first impression to follow-up. For businesses that want to grow, the goal is not just to generate more attention. The goal is to make sure the system behind the attention is strong enough to support the customer when they are ready to move forward. Graphic direction: Create a LOGIC Marketing Systems static post with the headline “Trust Needs Infrastructure.” Use a modern connected-system visual showing Website, CRM, Email, Ads, Lead Follow-Up, and Reporting linked into one controlled customer journey. Keep the design clean, technical, premium, and business-friendly, with a polished systems architecture feel.
